Polymarket just crossed $1B+ in annualized revenue.

One interesting detail from CNBC's article is that Polymarket's U.S. exchange is only just getting started.

The platform was waitlisted until six weeks ago, still has no desktop version, and users have to download the app to trade. Despite those limitations, daily volume has already grown from around $50M to over $200M.

If this level of activity is possible before a full desktop launch, it'll be interesting to see how much further the platform can scale.

Today I made $100,000+ predicting peace between US and Iran

This was a 2 month long emotional roller coaster of US and Iran's mix of diplomacy and war.

We were close to a permanent peace deal 2 weeks ago, but then Trump pulled out trying to get a better deal.

On Saturday we finally saw signs of the talks being in the final stages:
> Qatar flew to Tehran to hammer out a deal during the weekend
> Trump hinted at a deal

But then Israel struck Beirut in Lebanon. The price crashed from 25% to 8%. I was down -$20,000 on all of my peace positions. But after a lot of thinking I realized this could be a sign Israel knows the deal is happening and tries to sabotage it. So I doubled down at around 10%.

A few hours later the deal got announced and I made $100K.

This is my second biggest win. My biggest win was the initial US-Iran ceasefire ($100K+)

It's always great to bet on peace. I think in total I made $300K+ predicting various ceasefires in the Middle East
The best and richest oil traders I know are watching live Iranian state media instead of the World Cup.

Today, we're about to witness the biggest IPO in history.

SpaceX is targeting a $1.77T valuation at $135/share, enough to surpass Tesla's current ~$1.6T market cap and become the 7th largest company in the U.S.

The IPO has garnered a lot of hype and attention recently, but what unfolds next will be very interesting.

Looking back, history shows that even the largest IPOs can take very different paths:

> Visa (2008) was the largest IPO in U.S. history at the time, debuting with a market cap of approximately $44B. Price rallied 30% on Day 1 despite launching during the global financial crisis, then gained another 90% over the following 3 months.

> Alibaba (2014) then became the largest IPO in history, debuting with a market cap of approximately $231B. Price surged 45% on debut before falling 16% over the next 2 weeks, only to rally another 45% within the following month.

> Meta (2012) had one of the worst starts of any Top 10 company. The company debuted with a market cap of approximately $104B, making it the largest tech IPO ever at the time. Price fell 16% in the first week, 47% down within 3 months, and investors were still down 33% after the first year. Yet despite the brutal start, Meta eventually became one of the best performing mega IPOs ever.

If you bought these stocks at IPO and held til today:

• Visa: +2900%
• Meta: +1400%
• Alibaba: +39%

So where does SpaceX fit in all this? As we can see, demand is HUGE rn.

The IPO is reportedly more than 4x oversubscribed, with over $70B of retail capital waiting in allocation queues. A lot of retail have even dumped their other holdings for this IPO.

Hyperliquid's pre-IPO market currently values SpaceX at ~$2.26T ($174/share), while Polymarket traders are pricing its IPO closing market cap at ~$2.181T.

That in itself is more than $400B above the IPO valuation.

Could SpaceX follow the path of Visa, Meta, or Alibaba?
